claim: Currier A and Currier B use two structurally distinct cipher alphabets encoding the same underlying Latin plaintext: the two sub-corpora should exhibit the same bigram entropy and the same Zipf exponent once glyph-level correspondences are remapped
evidence_for: High-confidence belief (0.694) that A and B use distinct cipher tables on the same language
Currier B is more than twice the size of A (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with one scribe being primary
Positional glyph constraints are present in both sub-corpora, suggesting shared underlying grammar
evidence_against: If Zipf exponents diverge significantly between A and B after remapping, the same-language assumption is undermined
Differing entropy values per section could reflect genuine lexical differences between two scribes writing about different topics
test: Compute bigram entropy and Zipf exponent separately for Currier A and Currier B token sets; then apply an optimal glyph-to-glyph mapping (maximize token overlap) and recompute both metrics on the remapped B corpus
test_metric: KL-divergence between A and remapped-B unigram distributions; Zipf exponent difference
test_threshold: Confirmation if KL-divergence < 0.05 and Zipf exponent difference < 0.05 after remapping; denial if KL-divergence > 0.2

tests_run.test: compute bigram entropy and zipf exponent separately for currier a and currier b token sets; then app
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.
